name: Anol Bhattacherjee
bio: Anol Bhattacherjee is a Professor of Information Systems at the University of South Florida. He is best known for developing the Expectation-Confirmation Model of IS Continuance (ECM), which explains why users continue or discontinue using information systems after initial adoption. His work bridges consumer behaviour (Oliver's Expectation-Confirmation Theory) with information systems research, focusing on post-adoption behaviour.

keyArticles.citation: Bhattacherjee, A. (2001). Understanding information systems continuance: An expectation-confirmation model. MIS Quarterly, 25(3), 351–370.
keyArticles.description: Foundational paper introducing ECM. Extended Oliver's ECT to IS context, adding perceived usefulness and replacing repurchase intention with continuance intention.

name: Fred Davis
bio: Fred D. Davis is a Distinguished Professor Emeritus at the University of Arkansas. He proposed the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) in his 1986 PhD dissertation at MIT, which was formally published in MIS Quarterly in 1989. TAM has become one of the most widely cited and applied models in information systems and technology adoption research. Davis adapted Fishbein and Ajzen's Theory of Reasoned Action to focus specifically on system acceptance.

keyArticles.citation: Davis, F. D. (1989). Perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, and user acceptance of information technology. MIS Quarterly, 13(3), 319–340.
keyArticles.description: Foundational TAM paper establishing perceived usefulness (PU) and perceived ease of use (PEOU) as key predictors of behavioral intention and technology adoption.

name: Icek Ajzen
bio: Icek Ajzen is Professor Emeritus of Psychology at the University of Massachusetts Amherst. With Martin Fishbein he developed the Theory of Reasoned Action (TRA), which links attitudes and subjective norms to behavioral intentions and behavior. He later proposed the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), adding perceived behavioral control to account for actions that are not fully under volitional control. TRA and TPB are cornerstones of social psychology and have strongly influenced technology acceptance research—Fred Davis explicitly built TAM on Fishbein and Ajzen’s work.

keyContributions.title: Theory of Reasoned Action (with Martin Fishbein)

keyArticles.citation: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
keyArticles.description: Foundational journal article presenting TPB: intention is predicted by attitude, subjective norm, and perceived behavioral control; intention and PBC predict behavior when control is realistic.

keyArticles.citation: Fishbein, M., & Ajzen, I. (1975). Belief, attitude, intention, and behavior: An introduction to theory and research. Reading, MA: Addison-Wesley.
keyArticles.description: Classic monograph developing the Theory of Reasoned Action and the expectancy–value model of attitudes, forming the basis for later work on planned behavior.

name: Viswanath Venkatesh
bio: Viswanath Venkatesh is a Distinguished Professor at the University of Arkansas. He is a leading researcher in technology adoption and use, best known for the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT, 2003) and UTAUT2 (2012). Venkatesh and colleagues consolidated eight competing models of technology acceptance into UTAUT, significantly advancing the field. He has also contributed to TAM2 (with Davis) and various extensions for consumer and voluntary contexts.

keyContributions.title: Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT)

keyArticles.citation: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425–478.
keyArticles.description: Seminal paper consolidating TAM, TPB, and other models into UTAUT. Identifies performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating conditions as core determinants.

keyArticles.citation: Venkatesh, V., Thong, J. Y. L., & Xu, X. (2012). Consumer acceptance and use of information technology: Extending the unified theory of acceptance and use of technology. MIS Quarterly, 36(1), 157–178.
keyArticles.description: UTAUT2 extends UTAUT for voluntary consumer contexts, adding hedonic motivation, price value, and habit.

keyArticles.citation: Venkatesh, V., & Davis, F. D. (2000). A theoretical extension of the technology acceptance model: Four longitudinal field studies. Management Science, 46(2), 186–204.
keyArticles.description: TAM2 introduces social influencing processes and cognitive instrumental processes as determinants of perceived usefulness.

name: Marlize Terblanche-Smit
bio: Marlize Terblanche-Smit is a Professor of Marketing at Stellenbosch Business School (USB), where she has been based since 2019. She previously served as Associate Professor at Stellenbosch University (2013–2018) and was head of the MBA programme at USB from 2014. Her research focuses on marketing strategy, branding, digital marketing, consumer behaviour, and non-profit marketing. Her institutional profile is tagged with Responsible Leadership. Stellenbosch Business School holds AACSB, EQUIS and AMBA accreditation.

name: Nicky Terblanche
bio: Nicky Terblanche is an Associate Professor of Leadership Coaching at Stellenbosch Business School and Head of the MPhil Leadership Coaching and PhD programmes. He holds a PhD in Business Management and an MSc in Electronic Engineering. His research focuses on transition coaching, transformative learning, social network analysis, complexity theory, and the application of AI and machine learning in coaching. He has developed an AI Bot Coach called Vici and researches how newly appointed senior leaders can be supported during career transitions.

keyArticles.citation: Terblanche, N., Albertyn, R., & Van Coller-Peter, S. (2020). Why newly appointed senior leaders need support. Stellenbosch Business School Management Review.
keyArticles.description: Explores organisational support for newly promoted leaders during critical transitions.

name: Charlene Gerber
bio: Charlene Gerber is a researcher in the Department of Business Management at Stellenbosch University. Her work spans social media in recruitment, behavioral finance, and student engagement. Her highly cited paper on social media and recruitment (SA Journal of Human Resource Management, 2018) has received hundreds of citations. She has also published on familiarity bias amongst individual investors and longitudinal studies of academic performance.

keyArticles.citation: Gerber, C., & Schlechter, C. (2018). The impact of social media on recruitment: Are you LinkedIn? SA Journal of Human Resource Management, 16, a861.
keyArticles.description: Examines how social media platforms influence recruitment practices and employer branding.

name: Richard Heeks
bio: Richard Heeks is Professor of Development Informatics at the University of Manchester and a leading scholar in ICT4D (Information and Communication Technology for Development). His work focuses on how digital technologies shape development outcomes in low- and middle-income countries, including issues of digital exclusion, e-government, and development failures and success factors. He founded the Centre for Digital Development (formerly Centre for Development Informatics) and has written extensively on how context and institutions shape ICT projects in the Global South.

keyArticles.citation: Heeks, R. (2002). Information systems and developing countries: Failure, success, and local improvisations. The Information Society, 18(2), 101–112.
keyArticles.description: Explores why many ICT projects in developing countries fail and highlights the importance of contextual fit, local improvisation, and institutional factors.

keyArticles.citation: Heeks, R. (2010). Do information and communication technologies (ICTs) contribute to development? Journal of International Development, 22(5), 625–640.
keyArticles.description: Reviews the evidence base on ICT4D and proposes a more nuanced understanding of how ICTs contribute to development outcomes.

name: Geoff Walsham
bio: Geoff Walsham is Emeritus Professor of Management Studies at the University of Cambridge Judge Business School. He is widely recognised for pioneering interpretive research in information systems, studying how ICTs are socially constructed and embedded within organisations and societies. His work often focuses on ICTs, globalisation, and development, including research on ICT use in Africa and Asia, and on cross-cultural aspects of IS implementation.

keyArticles.citation: Walsham, G. (1995). Interpretive case studies in IS research: Nature and method. European Journal of Information Systems, 4(2), 74–81.
keyArticles.description: Seminal methodological paper outlining the interpretive case study approach in information systems research.

keyArticles.citation: Walsham, G. (2017). ICT4D research: Reflections on history and future agenda. Information Technology for Development, 23(1), 18–41.
keyArticles.description: Reflects on the evolution of ICT4D research and proposes future directions, with a focus on power, inequality, and context.
